name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit

summary.name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
summary.hypothesis: The grouped-CTA traversal retune just lost 0.341504 ms versus the 24.177664 ms accepted PTX base, which is a strong signal that CTA ordering is not the next high-leverage knob on this branch. The active kernel is still the PTX 128x128 hot-band microkernel at 200 registers per thread with only 16.60% active warps and 48.36% tensor-pipe activity, while DRAM remains low at 9.73%. The next best move is therefore to stay on the winning PTX branch and retune the inner control/export surface that actually governs issue efficiency: copy/consume order, helper lifetime, and export sequencing inside the live microkernel, not another grid-traversal experiment.
summary.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the current 128x128 microkernel, not global bandwidth or another CTA-ordering miss.
summary.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best measured family and touches the actual hot path, but PTX control-surface tweaks can still collapse into noise or silently grow live ranges if the scope is not bounded.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.177664 ms base
ranking_notes: Round 4 already falsified grouped-CTA traversal as the primary next family on top of the current PTX winner.
This is the most coherent path that keeps the best-performing branch alive while targeting the still register-limited hot kernel.
It remains ahead of the historical restores because it attacks the live branch directly instead of rolling back to an older near-best state.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if another bounded PTX-local pass also leaves register count, active warps, and hot-band time effectively unchanged or loses cleanly against the 24.177664 ms base.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
